name: Confirmation Bias
summary: The tendency to seek, interpret, and remember information that confirms what you already believe — while discounting evidence to the contrary.
example: An investor who is bullish on a stock only reads articles supporting the buy thesis, and dismisses bearish analysis as 'noise'.

name: Sunk Cost Fallacy
summary: The tendency to keep investing in a decision because of past investment, even when future costs outweigh future benefits.
example: Continuing to pour money into a failing project because you've 'already spent so much' on it.

name: Anchoring Bias
summary: Over-reliance on the first piece of information offered (the 'anchor') when making subsequent judgments.
example: A first salary offer of 80k anchors the negotiation, even though comparable roles pay 110k.

name: Availability Heuristic
summary: Judging the probability of events by how easily examples come to mind, rather than by actual frequency.
example: Fearing plane crashes more than car accidents because plane crashes are vivid in the news, despite cars being statistically more dangerous.

name: Survivorship Bias
summary: Focusing on the people or things that 'survived' a process and ignoring those that didn't, leading to false conclusions.
example: Studying only successful startups to learn 'what works', ignoring the thousands that did the same things and failed.

name: Loss Aversion
summary: The tendency to prefer avoiding losses over acquiring equivalent gains — losing 100 hurts about twice as much as gaining 100 feels good.
example: Holding a losing stock too long to 'avoid realizing the loss', while quickly selling winners.

name: Hindsight Bias
summary: The 'I-knew-it-all-along' effect — perceiving past events as having been more predictable than they actually were.
example: After a startup fails, claiming 'it was obvious from the beginning' — even though you invested in it.

name: Overconfidence Effect
summary: Excessive confidence in one's own answers, abilities, or predictions, beyond what evidence justifies.
example: Claiming '90% confidence' in a forecast that, calibrated against history, deserves at most 60%.

name: Authority Bias
summary: The tendency to attribute greater accuracy and weight to the opinion of an authority figure, independent of its actual content.
example: Following a famous CEO's strategic advice without checking if it actually applies to your context.

name: Social Proof
summary: Doing something because many others are doing it, treating popularity as evidence of correctness.
example: Buying a stock because it's trending on social media, not because the fundamentals support it.

name: Halo Effect
summary: A single positive trait (attractiveness, eloquence, fame) bleeds over and biases the evaluation of unrelated qualities.
example: Assuming a charismatic founder is also a competent operator, without checking execution track record.

name: Action Bias
summary: The urge to act — to 'do something' — even when waiting or doing nothing would lead to a better outcome.
example: A goalkeeper diving left or right on penalties even though statistically staying in the center saves more goals.

name: Endowment Effect
summary: Valuing something more highly simply because you own it, beyond its market value.
example: Refusing to sell a car for its fair market price because 'it's worth more to me' — without a concrete reason.

name: Recency Bias
summary: Over-weighting recent events and under-weighting older data when making predictions or decisions.
example: Assuming a stock will keep going up because it has rallied for three months, ignoring its 10-year volatility.

name: Status Quo Bias
summary: A preference for the current state of affairs — change is perceived as loss, even when change would be objectively better.
example: Sticking with an underperforming vendor 'because switching is too risky', without quantifying the risk.

name: Planning Fallacy
summary: Systematically underestimating the time, cost, and risk of future projects while overestimating the benefits.
example: Promising to ship a feature in two weeks when comparable past projects have always taken six.

name: Dunning-Kruger Effect
summary: People with limited knowledge in a domain often overestimate their competence; experts often underestimate theirs.
example: After reading two articles on machine learning, confidently telling a researcher their model is 'wrong'.

name: Bandwagon Effect
summary: Adopting beliefs or behaviors because many others have — independent of underlying evidence.
example: Joining a startup 'because everyone in the network is moving into AI', without evaluating fit.
